title: Last Stone Weight (max-heap)
code: foreach (int s in stones) heap.Enqueue(s, s);
if (a != b) heap.Enqueue(a - b, a - b);

frames.caption: Goal: repeatedly smash the two heaviest stones; the leftover (heavier - lighter) goes back. One stone (or none) remains.
frames.note: This is a greedy problem: at every step the right move is "take the two current heaviest." A max-heap is the data structure behind that greedy — "always take the current best" is exactly "pop the root."

frames.caption: Build the max-heap from all 6 stones. Root 8 is the heaviest.
frames.note: Load every stone into a MAX-heap. A binary heap is a complete tree packed in an array with one invariant — every parent >= its children (max-heap) — so the heaviest stone is always at the root, peekable in O(1). Building it costs O(n) with Floyd bottom-up (or O(n log n) by n separate pushes); after this, "the current heaviest" is just heap[0].

frames.caption: heap.Count = 6 > 1: another round. Pop the two heaviest.
frames.note: The loop runs while the heap holds more than one stone — each round removes two and adds at most one, so the heap strictly shrinks and the process must terminate. n rounds x O(log n) per pop/push gives O(n log n) overall, O(n) space for the heap.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root: a = 8 (heaviest).
frames.note: Dequeue returns the root — the single heaviest stone — then re-heapifies by moving the last leaf to the root and sifting it down past the larger child until the parent>=children invariant holds again. That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.caption: a = 8 removed; heap re-heapified, new root 7.
frames.note: Dequeue returns the root — the single heaviest stone — then re-heapifies by moving the last leaf to the root and sifting it down past the larger child until the parent>=children invariant holds again. That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root again: b = 7 (next heaviest).
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: b = 7 removed. Smash a=8 against b=7.
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: a != b: enqueue remainder a - b = 1. heap.Count = 5.
frames.note: The stones differ, so smashing leaves a remainder of a-b. Enqueue it: append at the next free leaf and sift it UP past smaller parents until the max-heap invariant holds — another O(log n). The remainder rejoins the contest and may be smashed again in a later round.

frames.caption: heap.Count = 5 > 1: another round. Pop the two heaviest.
frames.note: The loop runs while the heap holds more than one stone — each round removes two and adds at most one, so the heap strictly shrinks and the process must terminate. n rounds x O(log n) per pop/push gives O(n log n) overall, O(n) space for the heap.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root: a = 4 (heaviest).
frames.note: Dequeue returns the root — the single heaviest stone — then re-heapifies by moving the last leaf to the root and sifting it down past the larger child until the parent>=children invariant holds again. That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.note: Dequeue returns the root — the single heaviest stone — then re-heapifies by moving the last leaf to the root and sifting it down past the larger child until the parent>=children invariant holds again. That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root again: b = 2 (next heaviest).
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: b = 2 removed. Smash a=4 against b=2.
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: a != b: enqueue remainder a - b = 2. heap.Count = 4.
frames.note: The stones differ, so smashing leaves a remainder of a-b. Enqueue it: append at the next free leaf and sift it UP past smaller parents until the max-heap invariant holds — another O(log n). The remainder rejoins the contest and may be smashed again in a later round.

That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.caption: b = 1 removed. Smash a=2 against b=1.
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: a != b: enqueue remainder a - b = 1. heap.Count = 3.
frames.note: The stones differ, so smashing leaves a remainder of a-b. Enqueue it: append at the next free leaf and sift it UP past smaller parents until the max-heap invariant holds — another O(log n). The remainder rejoins the contest and may be smashed again in a later round.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root: a = 1 (heaviest).

frames.caption: a = 1 removed; heap re-heapified, new root 1.

That sift-down touches at most floor(log2 n) levels, so popping the max is O(log n). This is exactly why a heap beats a plain array here: a scan would be O(n) every round.

frames.caption: Dequeue the root again: b = 1 (next heaviest).
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: b = 1 removed. Smash a=1 against b=1.
frames.note: Dequeue the new root — the SECOND heaviest. Pulling the two largest is two O(log n) pops, not an O(n) two-pass scan. This greedy choice ("always smash the two heaviest") is what a max-heap is built to serve: "take the current best" is literally "pop the root."

frames.caption: a == b = 1: both stones destroyed, nothing returns. heap.Count = 1.
frames.note: The two heaviest are equal, so they annihilate completely — nothing goes back. Both stones are gone and the heap shrinks by two. No enqueue this round.

frames.caption: heap.Count == 1: one stone of weight 1 survives. Return 1.
frames.note: At most one stone is left. If exactly one remains it is the answer (its weight); if the heap is empty every stone was annihilated, so the answer is 0. Here a single stone of weight 1 survives — that is the last stone weight.
